Networks around all Colombia, cool.

Crazy. All right, that's it. Should I begin? Let's do it.
Okay, let me present my screen. You see the whiteboard, right?
Yes. Yep. Okay, so usually I began introducing machine learning using an example which I personally like the most. It sets the context.
And then we'll talk about the taxonomy of machine learning, some of the main branches and sub-branches, and then we'll move towards algorithms.
Afterwards, we'll go into the rest of things.
The way machine learning works: the first thing is how traditionally people used to use computers in order to solve a problem.
What they would do is they would write a rule for the task that they like to perform.
The rule is hardcoded into the computer, and then we have some input data coming into the computer.
That rule, whatever it is, processes that input data and produces an output.
So, just for example, the rule could be: whatever comes in, just square it.
Let's say the input is X. Inside the computer, we have written a program which squares the input, and then it produces the output, which is Y.
So if I have one, the output is one. If I have two, the output is four.
And so on. Now, as you can see, the rule is hardcoded here, and we just are feeding some data into the computer, and it's producing output.
The way this paradigm has now changed with machine learning is we would like this computer to learn the rule for itself.
We're not going to hardcode that rule, but what we are going to do is we are going to provide it some historical data of inputs and outputs.
And it is the task of this computer program to learn the rule, to learn the mapping between that input and output.
So, for instance, if we take this example:
At the beginning, the program doesn't know if the relation is a squared relation or whatever it is.
What we do is provide an input and a corresponding output based on some historical data, and it is the job of this program or computer to learn the mapping between that input and output.
So the input could be 1, 2, 3 and the output is 1, 4, 9.
Once it learns that mapping between input and output, we can then make use of this learned rule in order to produce an output.
I can take this learned rule and then I next provide it an input, and because it has learned the mapping between input and output, it would be producing me an output.
For instance, if I feed it—what I want from this learned rule is to produce something like 16, because we know that the input value provided here is just a squaring relationship.
We know that right now because I deliberately put this example.
But what we'd like to do is even though we don't know this rule ourselves, we would still like this computer to learn that rule for itself.
A typical example would be that the input could be an image of something, and the corresponding output could be what is inside that image.
Let's say this image is of a cat.
We provide an image and provide the corresponding label that it's a cat.
We provide lots and lots of such images, and then what we'd like is for this rule to have learned that next time I provide you an image, you should recognize if it's a cat or not.
As you can see, we're not explicitly writing any rules for it, and probably writing rules for such a kind of problem is even impossible.
We know that an image is just a bunch of pixel values, and there's no way you can hardcode to tell a computer what a cat looks like.
For instance, you can define that if this pixel value is this much and that pixel value is that much and so on, maybe that's a cat.
But that would be just for this image.
What if the pose of the cat changes?
What if the lighting changes?
What if the view changes?
What if the background changes?
You'd have to write the entire code, entire rule for different images again.
This kind of thing tells you or shows you the importance or the significance of
How machine learning is changing this paradigm—we are now not worried about hard-coding things ourselves.
We can just leverage historical data in order to learn this mapping and then make use of it in the future.
Okay, I have these few slides.
Although we would not be using slides in the course, I personally find them boring, but this is just because we are introducing lots of taxonomy here.
That's why it would be easier for me to quickly go through them using slides.
Feel free to interrupt me anywhere.
Although we're not going to do in-depth discussions about each of the sub-branches, I'll just be introducing you, and then we'll cover each of them in future sessions.
So broadly, do like a presentation mode so we can see it a little bit larger.
Sure, is it working now? Yeah, perfect.
So machine learning is broadly categorized into three fields: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning.

Although these days you can draw another arrow here and write generative learning as well because of its recent emergence, but traditionally these are the major branches that machine learning has been studied.
Generative learning is still in its infancy and is heavily under research, with lots of different algorithms coming every day.
That's why it would be difficult to produce a nice-looking taxonomy for it.
Maybe we'll touch a little bit on generative learning towards the end of this course, but the goal here is to introduce you to the fundamentals.
So now, what is the basis on which do we have these divisions?
This division is mainly based on how you present your data to the learning algorithm.
Supervised learning: when the data is nicely labeled, meaning when you have features and also the corresponding labels, which can be thought of as questions and answers.
The example that I provided where we had x 1, 2, 3, y 1, 4, 9—we had inputs and we have corresponding outputs.
This kind of dataset is called labeled data because you have features and you have labels.
Features are whatever input you have and labels are the target.
A variable that you'd like to predict.
If you have your data labeled, another term that is used for it is annotated data.
If you have annotated data, supervised learning is the branch to go to in order to do some machine learning on it.
Now supervised learning is further divided into two sub-branches: one is regression, another is classification.
In regression, the goal is to predict a continuous variable—it could be the temperature tomorrow or the stock price, or anything which varies continuously.
There's nothing that was discretized in nature, so that kind of work is handled in regression.
In classification, the target variable is categorized—it is discrete in nature.
You already know what the potential output of the learning algorithm could be: one of the categories.
The categories are known to us already, and that's why it is called classification.
The second branch is unsupervised learning, where the data that is presented to the learning algorithm is not labeled data.
You have a bunch of data that is available, but it's not annotated.
For instance, you can have lots and lots of images, but you don't know what labels are inside those images, or in other words, you can have a bunch of news articles or text articles.
But you don't know what those articles are about.
Now, if you have data available to you in this way, the question that comes to mind is: what can we do with such data?
One thing that we can do with such data is recognize similar-looking images together or similar articles together which talk about the same topic.
That kind of thing is called clustering, where you have a bunch of data available to you and the goal is to recognize similar-looking things together.
That is handled in clustering, which comes under unsupervised learning.
Another task that is usually performed in unsupervised learning is called dimensionality reduction.
Dimensionality reduction is the task where you have data regarding something and there are lots of features, and maybe not all of them are important, or maybe the size is huge.
You'd like to compress it to a reduced set of features, but you would like to do it in a way where you prevent
Prevent yourself from throwing away important information.
You throw away information which is least important.
Dimension reduction is what deals with that.
We'll see this in detail.
There are mathematical tools for doing this dimensionality reduction.
It comes from linear algebra.
That's what you can usually do in unsupervised learning.
Reinforcement learning, on the other hand, works differently from the first two.
In reinforcement learning, we don't have prior data available to us.
What we have is what is called an agent and an environment.
We have defined an environment and we place an agent inside that environment.
We have defined actions for that agent.
Based on those actions, if an agent does something, we control its behavior by providing it what is called a reward function.
If the agent is doing something that is desirable, we provide a positive reward.
If it is doing something that is not desirable, we provide a negative reward.
A typical example would be a car driving itself on a road.
If it is moving on the road without crashing, maybe that's something that we would like it to do, so we provide a positive reward.
If it crashes, we provide a negative reward.
In order to prevent it from doing it in the next session.
Reinforcement learning is not very much industrialized.
The majority of what is happening is still under research, although there have been some recent developments from Google DeepMind.
They're leveraging reinforcement learning techniques to do research in the medical field.
If you have heard the terms protein unfolding, they're now even using it to solve the first thing they did, which was protein unfolding.
This was one of the toughest problems in biological sciences.
But now they are training it to learn to design drugs, which previously took years.
Now they are claiming that it is handling them in a matter of seconds or minutes.
Reinforcement learning is a very interesting field of research.
We would not be focusing too much on reinforcement learning in this course because we are focusing more on the fundamentals of AI.
And once you learn those fundamentals, those techniques are what you'll use.
would be used in such reinforcement learning.
For instance, when we cover neural networks, a sub-branch of reinforcement learning called Deep Reinforcement Learning is using neural networks in order to do things.
At least I would provide you a base around if you would like to further study reinforcement learning, you would be good to go.
Any questions?
For the dimensionality reduction, is there a specific type of dataset that works better, or what kind of datasets would you want to use that on?
Usually it works for table datasets, but you can perform dimensional reduction on even images as well if you arrange the pixels in a table way.
Although this is something I haven't done myself, but now since you've asked, I think you can do that for pixels as well.
But usually, most of what I've seen, dimensional reduction is used when you have some table of data and you would like to reduce the set of features.

For instance, one of the problems that will be coming in classification, which is logistic regression, we have a dataset about tumors with tumor size, the radius of it, and lots of other medical terms.

We can use dimensionality reduction first to use all of the features and see how the algorithm is performing, and then we can do dimensionality reduction to reduce the set of features and see how it is impacting the performance of the algorithm.

If the performance is not reduced much and we have still reduced the number of features, that would be a good thing to do because it would reduce the computational burden on the computer and also compress the data in a nicer way.
I have a question on this: is dimensionality reduction mostly used for optimization or is there also some other use cases?
This is usually utilized in places where there's a term called curse of dimensionality.
Sometimes you have so many features that the computational space just blows up.

And the available compute resources that you have are not enough to care for that, and in those situations, usually it is advisable to do dimensionality reduction first to reduce the set of features, and then make use of them in order to see if it is working.
That's one use case, particularly what is used mostly in machine learning for dimensionality reduction, but there could be some use cases of it even outside standard machine learning.

For instance, if you'd like to do compression because the storage size that the data is taking is huge, you maybe would like to compress data, but one thing that is very important to keep in mind is dimensionality reduction is not reversible.
Once you take these features and you compress them into this low dimensionality, the information about individual features is lost.

For instance, if you have one feature as radius, another is maybe the texture of the tumor, and so on, and I have reduced them into, let's say, two sets of features, which is lower than what the original number of features were, that information is now lost.

Now I don't know what that feature is representing, although it is important and a learning algorithm can make use of it to make useful decisions, but I don't know what that feature is anymore because the semantic meaning in that feature is lost.
I have a question: so the way I think about unsupervised learning anyway, I think one kind of branch of that is embedding. Does that fall more into clustering or dimensionality reduction?
So embeddings, I think you're referring to word embeddings, where we have assigned these vectors to each word in a text, where the vector is tasked to learn the semantic meaning in a word.

Usually these algorithms, the language models that are trained, there's a concept called semi-supervised learning, so it is a recent term, but what happens in semi-supervised learning is you take, let's say you have defined, the word dimension of words is 32, for each word you have assigned 32 dimensions.
Vectors: the way you train it, you take a bunch of text and randomly remove words from it.
Then you task your language model, which could be a Transformer or an LSTM or whatever you're using, to produce or predict the next word that you have masked deliberately.
In that way, the word embeddings are learned.
If you'd like to categorize that in this picture here, there should be another arrow calling it self-supervised learning.
That is a more relevant term, but just as I said, these concepts are relatively new, so I haven't deliberately put them in the slide, but you can write another with self-supervised learning.
There's another term as well called semisupervised learning.
In semisupervised learning, sometimes you have a gigantic amount of data.
Not labeling all of it or annotating all of it is very difficult, so what you do is you take a certain chunk of that data, manually label it, annotate it, and train a learning algorithm on it.
Then you take the rest of the data and make predictions for the rest of the data.
Because the algorithm is trained and whatever labels it produced, now the whole data is labeled, the whole data is annotated.
Then you take the whole data again and retrain the entire algorithm.
This concept is called semisupervised learning.

Now, of course, the question that comes to mind is that if you have trained the algorithm on a reduced set of data in the first place, it is going to generate some errors, of course, but researchers have shown that the benefit is still more than the errors introduced.
So semisupervised learning also works sometimes.
Any more questions?
I could have one more, but if we're running low on time, we can keep going to next.
Okay, so let's move on.
The next thing that I would like to talk about is a standard workflow of a machine learning model.
Did you hear that he had one more question?
No, we can keep going. I can ask at the end.
Finishing okay, probably it's okay.

So a standard workflow of a machine learning project usually involves these steps, although there would be projects which would not follow the standard linear procedure, but still it is good to keep in mind.
The story begins with data.
Usually, machine learning engineers begin at this stage, so the data is available to them.
They don't usually worry about where the data is coming from.
It could be stored in Excel sheets, some database, coming from a live API, or something, but we have the data.
The next step is what is called EDA, or exploratory data analysis.
Once you have the data available to you, what you would like to do is to get an insight of what the data looks like, if there are any inconsistencies in the data, or any outliers.
You can also do your own analysis, for instance, correlation analysis.
You'd like to see how features are related to each other, and that kind of stuff.
EDA is a stage where these kinds of things are usually handled.
Once you get an insight of your data, the next step is preprocessing the data.
Each learning algorithm likes the data to be presented to it in a certain shape and format.
For instance, if there are images, you cannot just simply throw images of different sizes at a neural network.
You'd have to reshape them into a fixed size and normalize them.
We'll talk about these things in future sessions, but these are just some of the things.

Or if it is tabular data and maybe there are lots of missing entries in there, you would like to take care of those, or outliers you'd like to take care of those before you feed that data to the learning algorithm.
These kinds of things are usually handled at the preprocessing stage.
Once the data is ready, the next step is model designing.

Model designing is a term more relevant to neural networks because you usually have a lot of choices for the architecture, how many layers you would like to use, how many neurons you want to put in each layer, what kind of optimization algorithm you'd like to use.
All those kinds of things, so this is what
Is usually handled at the model designing phase.
Once the model is designed, the next step is then to train the model.
One thing that I didn't talk about in the pre-processing is that we usually create two sets in it: what are called the training and test set.
What we simply do is that we do not take the entire data and feed it to the learning algorithm in order to train.
We set aside a certain chunk, a small chunk from it—let's say 20%—because we would like to test the performance of the algorithm once it is trained.
So we use 80% of the data to train the algorithm, and because we cannot just blindly deploy that model to the world, we would like to test its performance.
So then we bring about our set-aside test data and we evaluate the performance of the model.
That is handled in model evaluation.
If the performance of the model is satisfactory, that's good enough.
If it is not, you go back to the model designing phase, change the architecture, change the hyperparameters that are there.
Maybe sometimes you'd also like to pre-process data differently.
So this is a back and forth process here between model training, evaluation, designing, and pre-processing.
You spend a lot of time in these four steps.
Once the performance is satisfactory, you can then proceed towards the deployment stage.
So that's pretty much the end of a workflow.
Any questions?
So I've heard about the training set and the evaluation set.
I've heard of it in two different ways: one is the way you said where you have a training set and a test set, and then you use the test set to validate the model.
And then one other way is to have three sets instead: you have the training set, the evaluation set, and then the test set.
I guess what is the significance of the evaluation set and when should you use it versus not use it?
Yeah, so sometimes what happens is: suppose you're training a new network on a difficult problem.
You have set aside this test data, you are training the model on the training set and then evaluating it on the test set, but it's not working.
So you go back to the model designing and you tweak the
Model architecture: pre-process the data and then do the training and again do the evaluation.
Suppose you do this for, let's say, 20 or 30 number of times.
At the 30th number of time, the model is producing good results, so you can fool yourself into thinking that the model is good enough because it is learning from the data.

But what could have happened is the algorithm, instead of learning the general mapping between input and output, could have learned that it is particularly tuned towards that specific test data that you have set aside.
So it is biased toward that particular test data, but when you take the algorithm and deploy it into the real world, it is not producing the desired behavior.

In order to avoid this scenario, experts sometimes suggest that instead of creating two sets, you can create three sets: one is called the training set, another is called the validation set, and the third one is called the test set.
The percentage could be: 20% for testing, 10% for validation, and 70% for training.
You take your model, train it on the 70%, and evaluate it on the validation set.
If it is not performing good enough, you go back to the model designing phase, tweak the model again, and do the training and evaluation again on that 10%.
Maybe the model is now producing good results.
In order to put a final check on it, then you bring in your test data, the 20% test data you have set aside, and you check its performance on that.
That would double check that the model would be generalizable and would be producing good results on real world data as well.
So that is also something that is practiced by machine learning practitioners.
Isn't that called overfitting, what you just described, like when it's trained on a certain set and it's very well tuned for this set but it's not generalizing well to other data?
Yeah, so overfitting is a term that is mostly used in places where you have training data and you are training the model on it.
The model is producing very good results on the data that the model has seen during its training.
But when you bring in data
that it has not seen before it is producing poor results on that data is overfitting, but what Bren was talking about is a different phenomenon.

Even the test data that the model doesn't see during its training, but still the model is biased towards that data because you are tweaking the model over and over again in order to produce good results on that test data.
So even though it's not present in its training data, it is still biased toward that data.
One example could be: let's say the test data belongs to a particular region of the world, for instance, a particular part of the world where the data is coming from.
Now when you're doing this training and evaluation on this test data over and over again, maybe the model is biased toward that particular region of the world where the test data is coming from.
And then when you take the model and deploy it into the rest of the world, it is not producing good results.
So it is slightly different from overfitting because in overfitting the model is seeing that data itself while it is getting trained on that, but bias is slightly different from that.
So for ChatGPT and stuff like that, since they have infinitely different types of prompts that each user can do, how do they pre-process that data?
You don't know what the user will put till they send it, right?
Okay, so models like ChatGPT are trained in a different way.
Since you have asked the question, let me quickly talk about that.
So ChatGPT or any other LLMs are usually trained in a three-step process.
The first step is called self-supervised learning, which I was talking about.

So in the self-supervised stage, you just take a bunch of random articles or text from the internet and you randomly remove words from it, and you task your learning algorithm, which is typically a transformer, to produce that next word.
Once you do that training in that way, in a self-supervised learning way, the model that you come up with is what is called a foundation model, which is generative.
The model, like GPT-1, GPT-2, GPT-3, these are foundation
models.

So what they do is that when you provide it an input, it is just producing words one word at a time in an autoregressive fashion, but at this stage it is not a chatbot because it is just generating text.
That's where the second stage comes in, where you fine-tune it on question-answer pairs.
That happens in supervised learning, so you have a bunch of questions and a bunch of answers, and you take that question-answer pair and fine-tune the foundation model on those question-answers.
At that stage, the model that comes out of it can be treated as a chatbot because you're then putting in a question and it is producing an output.
But at this stage, the model is still not ready to be deployed because it could be producing harmful behaviors.
So that's why, in order to align it to human standards and make it civilized, we do a final step which is called reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF).

In the RLHF phase, what we do is we take the model, we provide it a question, the model produces an output, a human reviews that answer, and if they like the answer—for instance, if it doesn't contain any abusive words or any harmful behavior—they provide a positive reward, and if it does something that is not desirable, they provide a negative reward.
In this fashion, you further make the model aligned to human standards.
Now, I think the question that you asked was about pre-processing or something for models like these.
It's a huge process, so you cannot simplify it to what a typical learning algorithm would be doing.
Although essentially, on a broader level, this is what is happening, but there would be lots of nuances and details that you would need to take care of.
But at least these are the major steps that are usually employed while training an LLM.
Can you briefly touch on the difference between pre-training versus fine-tuning?
Okay, so pre-training the model: you take the model just like I said.
You have, let's say, an article containing some words.
You randomly mask some of the words from it—let's say this one and this one and maybe some other one.
You task this model, which again is typically a Transformer, to produce the next word.
Now, if you ask me how that happens, the process is very complex.
We might do it in a detailed lecture on how we train a Transformer.
I have simple single head attention models as well.
We can do an exercise where we train a model in this way, but on a journal level, you just randomly mask some of the words and you task this Transformer to produce the next word.
That is pre-training, which happens in a self-supervised fashion.

In fine-tuning, which I haven't personally done because I didn't have much data to do the self-supervised learning correctly, if that doesn't work correctly, training further fine-tuning it on question-answers wouldn't produce something that would be very fruitful, but the next step which is fine-tuning is: you take that foundation model which you have pre-trained using self-supervised learning.
Then you train it in a supervised learning fashion.
So there's a question that you put, which can be considered as a feature, and the model produces an output which you label as an answer.
Then you compare it with what is the true answer or what was available to you.
Then you optimize the model in such a way to produce outputs that are as close as possible to what the true output is.
That's fine-tuning, and once that happens, the next step is RLHF.

Next step: just like I said, learning algorithms are usually divided into these two categories.
One is traditional learning algorithms, and another one is deep learning algorithms.
Back in the days, this distinction was not made; neural networks were treated in the same way as traditional algorithms like SVMs.
But later on, after 2010, or more precisely 2012, after AlexNet.
Came into being, which won this ImageNet competition of recognizing images.

People saw that deep learning algorithms or new networks, when they become bigger, are producing results that were previously unheard of, particularly in computer vision and in natural language processing.
So that's why, because of their exceptional performance, deep learning algorithms or new networks, particularly, were then treated in a separate class called deep learning.
The word "deep" comes from the fact that you keep increasing the number of layers in a neural network, and the architecture becomes bigger and bigger.
In other words, they become deeper and deeper.
That's where the term "deep learning" comes from.
Traditional learning algorithms include support vector machines, decision trees, random forest, XGBoost, linear regression, logistic regression, and those kinds of things.
Deep learning algorithms include multi-layer perceptrons, which is just a feedforward neural network, convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, Transformers, and so on.
The packages that are used for coding, for traditional learning algorithms, usually scikit-learn is the library that is used.
Majority of the traditional algorithms are coded in Python.
They're also very good tools for pre-processing in Python, for doing data normalization, which maybe we'll be talking about in future sessions.
Train-test split and performance metrics like confusion matrix and so on are also available in scikit-learn.

You can make use of them, and then to code neural networks and create architectures, there are two popular frameworks that could be used: one is TensorFlow, which is from Google; another one is PyTorch, from Meta.
TensorFlow has a wider community and contains tools for a variety of stuff, like creating web apps—they have TensorFlow.js—and then for creating mobile apps they have TensorFlow Lite.
So the ecosystem is bigger.
PyTorch is mostly popular in the research community, so if you'd like to design new architectures and come up with something new, PyTorch provides extra flexibility.

Okay, so this is a major criticism from people outside AI on algorithms that it is taking humongous amounts of data in order to learn something.

And then when people from AI starts comparing it to a human brain, people say that a human brain can learn from small amounts of data while these algorithms need terabytes and petabytes of data in order to learn something fairly simple.
So the criticism is valid.

Of course, at this stage we don't have learning algorithms that could learn from small amounts of data, but still there are tools and techniques that are used in machine learning in order to work around this problem.
So I'll just talk about one particular solution which is called transfer learning.
For instance, you would like to create an image classifier to recognize between a horse and an elephant, but you don't have lots of images for horses and elephants.
What you can do is take a learning algorithm and train it on something else or with similar concept.
Like, you have data for cats versus dogs—you have huge amounts of data for it.
You can train algorithm on that, and then you can do transfer learning where you take the trained model and further fine-tune it for the task at hand, which is horses versus elephants classifier.
And it works.
So this is one thing that you can do.
Now we'll talk more about why it works when we talk about CNNs and the way filters are trained; that would make more sense, but this is just one way of working when you have less amounts of data.
Another thing that you can do is huge amounts of data are usually required when you're doing neural network training.
So if the problem is simple enough and maybe you wouldn't need a neural network, you can make use of traditional algorithms like SVM, which doesn't require huge amounts of data.
So it's another thing.
A third solution
You can generate the data thanks to these generative models.
So if you have a large language model, you can produce outputs from it and take those outputs and train a smaller model on it, and it works.
This is something I've seen done recently, so that's also something you can do.
But of course there's also research towards coming up with architectures that would learn from lesser amounts of data.
For instance, one of them is called physics-informed neural networks.
What they do is instead of taking a naive neural network, which doesn't know anything about physics, they combine differential equations into neural networks.

When you code these physics equations into the learning model and algorithm, it learns about physics, and maybe that way the learning algorithm would know more about our data and our world and wouldn't require these huge amounts of data.
Lots of cool things are happening in the machine learning space.
This is fair criticism of machine learning algorithms, but let's see what comes out of it.
